records.ety: [F., fr. OF. sausse, LL. salsa, properly, salt pickle, fr. L. salsus salted, salt, p. p. of salire to salt, fr. sal salt. See Salt, and cf. Saucer, Souse pickle, Souse to plunge.]

defs: A composition of condiments and appetizing ingredients eaten with food as a relish; especially, a dressing for meat or fish or for puddings; as, mint sauce; sweet sauce, etc.
quotes: High sauces and rich spices fetched from the Indies.

defs: Any garden vegetables eaten with meat.
quotes: Roots, herbs, vine fruits, and salad flowers . . . they dish up various ways, and find them very delicious sauce to their meats, both roasted and boiled, fresh and salt.

defs: Stewed or preserved fruit eaten with other food as a relish; as, apple sauce, cranberry sauce, etc.

records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Sauced (sst); p. pr. & vb. n. Saucing (s"sng).]

defs: To accompany with something intended to give a higher relish; to supply with appetizing condiments; to season; to flavor.
To cause to relish anything, as if with a sauce; to tickle or gratify, as the palate; to please; to stimulate; hence, to cover, mingle, or dress, as if with sauce; to make an application to.
quotes: Earth, yield me roots; Who seeks for better of thee, sauce his palate With thy most operant poison!

defs: To make poignant; to give zest, flavor or interest to; to set off; to vary and render attractive.
quotes: Then fell she to sauce her desires with threatenings.
Thou sayest his meat was sauced with thy upbraidings.

defs: To treat with bitter, pert, or tart language; to be impudent or saucy to.
quotes: I'll sauce her with bitter words.

defs: A soft crayon for use in stump drawing or in shading with the stump.
